title: Dog Sneezing Blood: Causes, Urgency, and Next Steps | Vet Triage
meta_desc: Dog sneezing blood? Learn about epistaxis, urgency levels, and why photos assist your vet in diagnosing the cause. Find out what to do next.
content_html:
Understanding Nasal Bleeding and Sneezing

Seeing your dog sneeze a spray of blood, known as epistaxis, can be very alarming. This occurs when blood vessels in the nasal passages are damaged. Common causes include foreign objects lodged in the nose (like grass seeds), fungal or bacterial infections, dental disease affecting the upper jaw, or more serious issues such as nasal tumors or blood clotting disorders.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for continuous sneezing with blood spray is generally categorized as Medium. While it is rarely an immediate life-threat if your dog is otherwise energetic and breathing comfortably, it does require a veterinary examination within 24 to 48 hours. However, if the bleeding is profuse and continuous, or if your dog has pale gums, lethargy, or difficulty breathing, you should seek emergency care immediately.

Why Taking a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a photo of the blood spray and your dog's nose is highly beneficial for your veterinarian. Documentation helps the vet determine the volume of blood loss, whether the discharge is unilateral (one nostril) or bilateral (both nostrils), and the specific color of the blood. This visual evidence provides critical clues that help the medical team prioritize your dog's case and choose the correct diagnostic tests.

faqs.question: How can I help my dog stop sneezing right now?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog as calm as possible to lower their blood pressure. You can try applying a cold compress to the bridge of the nose if they allow it, but do not force them if it causes more distress or sneezing.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog any human nasal spray?
faqs.answer: No, you should never administer human nasal sprays or medications to your dog without direct veterinary instruction, as many can be toxic or worsen the irritation.

faqs.question: What tests will the vet perform?
faqs.answer: The vet may recommend blood work to check for clotting issues, nasal swabs, X-rays of the snout and teeth, or a rhinoscopy to look inside the nasal passages with a small camera.
